content.text:
### Starting an Actor Run with Ad-hoc Webhooks (API)

Source: https://docs.apify.com/platform/integrations/webhooks/ad-hoc-webhooks

This endpoint allows you to start an Actor run and configure ad-hoc webhooks to be triggered upon specific run events. The webhooks are defined in a base64 encoded JSON array passed as the `webhooks` URL parameter.

```APIDOC
## GET /v2/acts/[ACTOR_ID]/runs

### Description
Starts an Actor run and configures ad-hoc webhooks for specific event types. The `webhooks` parameter must be a base64 encoded stringified JSON array of webhook definitions.

### Method
GET

### Endpoint
`https://api.apify.com/v2/acts/[ACTOR_ID]/runs?token=[YOUR_API_TOKEN]&webhooks=[AD_HOC_WEBHOOKS]`

### Parameters
#### Query Parameters
- **token** (string) - Required - Your Apify API token.
- **webhooks** (string) - Required - A base64 encoded stringified JSON array of webhook definitions. Each definition can include `eventTypes`, `requestUrl`, and optionally `payloadTemplate`.

### Request Example
```
https://api.apify.com/v2/acts/my-actor-id/runs?token=YOUR_API_TOKEN&webhooks=W1sib25jZUNvbnRleHR1YWxEb2N1bWVudFNldCI6W119%0A%0AW3sib25jZUNvbnRleHR1YWxEb2N1bWVudFNldCI6W119%0A%0A%
```

--------------------------------

### Ad-hoc Webhooks Configuration

Source: https://context7_llms

This section details how to configure ad-hoc webhooks when starting an Actor run via the Apify API. Ad-hoc webhooks are single-use and trigger once when a run reaches a specified state. They are defined using the `webhooks` URL parameter.

```APIDOC
## POST /v2/acts/[ACTOR_ID]/runs

### Description
Initiates an Actor run and configures ad-hoc webhooks to be triggered upon specific event types.

### Method
POST

### Endpoint
`https://api.apify.com/v2/acts/[ACTOR_ID]/runs`

### Parameters
#### Query Parameters
- **token** (string) - Required - Your Apify API token.
- **webhooks** (string) - Required - A base64 encoded stringified JSON array of webhook definitions.

### Request Body Example
*(This endpoint is typically called with query parameters, but a POST request body might be used for other run configurations.)*

### Response
*(Response details for initiating an Actor run. See Apify API documentation for specifics.)*

--- 

## Ad-hoc Webhook Definition Structure

### Description
Defines the configuration for a single ad-hoc webhook.

### Structure
```json
[
    {
        "eventTypes": ["ACTOR.RUN.FAILED"],
        "requestUrl": "https://example.com/run-failed"
    },
    {
        "eventTypes": ["ACTOR.RUN.SUCCEEDED"],
        "requestUrl": "https://example.com/run-succeeded",
        "payloadTemplate": "{\"hello\": \"world\", \"resource\":{{resource}}}"
    }
]
```

### Fields
- **eventTypes** (array of strings) - Required - An array of event types that will trigger this webhook. Example: `['ACTOR.RUN.FAILED']`.
- **requestUrl** (string) - Required - The URL where the webhook request will be sent.
- **payloadTemplate** (string) - Optional - A template string for customizing the webhook payload. It uses a JSON-like structure and supports variable interpolation. The resulting text must be a valid JSON object with string values only.
```

--------------------------------

### Starting an Actor Run with Ad-hoc Webhooks via API

Source: https://docs.apify.com/webhooks/ad-hoc-webhooks

Learn how to define and include ad-hoc webhooks when starting an Actor run using the Apify API. This allows you to receive notifications when a run reaches a specific state.

```APIDOC
## POST /v2/acts/[ACTOR_ID]/runs

### Description
Starts an Actor run and configures ad-hoc webhooks to be triggered upon specific run events.

### Method
POST

### Endpoint
`https://api.apify.com/v2/acts/[ACTOR_ID]/runs`

### Parameters
#### Query Parameters
- **token** (string) - Required - Your Apify API token.
- **webhooks** (string) - Required - A base64 encoded stringified JSON array of webhook definitions.

#### Request Body
(Optional) Actor input or configuration.

### Request Example
`https://api.apify.com/v2/acts/some-actor-id/runs?token=YOUR_API_TOKEN&webhooks=W3sib3ZlcmRyaXZlIjogW215X21ldGFkYXRhXSB9XQ%3D%3D`

### Response
#### Success Response (200 OK)
- **id** (string) - The ID of the started Actor run.
- **status** (string) - The initial status of the Actor run.

#### Response Example
```json
{
  "id": "some-run-id",
  "status": "RUNNING"
}
```

### Ad-hoc Webhook Definition Structure
When defining ad-hoc webhooks, use the following JSON structure, which should be stringified and then base64 encoded for the `webhooks` query parameter:

```json
[
  {
    "eventTypes": ["ACTOR.RUN.FAILED"],
    "requestUrl": "https://example.com/run-failed"
  },
  {
    "eventTypes": ["ACTOR.RUN.SUCCEEDED"],
    "requestUrl": "https://example.com/run-succeeded",
    "payloadTemplate": "{\"hello\": \"world\", \"resource\":{{resource}}}"
  }
]
```

**Fields:**
- **eventTypes** (array of strings) - Required - The event types that will trigger the webhook (e.g., `ACTOR.RUN.FAILED`, `ACTOR.RUN.SUCCEEDED`).
- **requestUrl** (string) - Required - The URL to send the webhook request to.
- **payloadTemplate** (string) - Optional - A template for the request body payload. You can use Liquid syntax and variables like `{{resource}}`.
- **idempotencyKey** (string) - Optional - A unique key to prevent duplicate webhooks in case of Actor restarts. It's recommended to use the Actor run ID.
```

--------------------------------

### Ad-hoc Webhook Definition JSON

Source: https://docs.apify.com/platform/integrations/webhooks/ad-hoc-webhooks

Example of a JSON array defining ad-hoc webhooks. Each object specifies event types to listen for and the URL to send the notification to. Supports optional payload templates for custom data.

```json
[
    {
        "eventTypes": ["ACTOR.RUN.FAILED"],
        "requestUrl": "https://example.com/run-failed"
    },
    {
        "eventTypes": ["ACTOR.RUN.SUCCEEDED"],
        "requestUrl": "https://example.com/run-succeeded",
        "payloadTemplate": "{\"hello\": \"world\", \"resource\":{{resource}}}"
    }
]
```

--------------------------------

### JSON Structure for Ad-hoc Webhook Definitions

Source: https://docs.apify.com/platform/integrations/webhooks/ad-hoc-webhooks

This JSON structure defines the parameters for ad-hoc webhooks, including `eventTypes` to specify when the webhook should trigger and `requestUrl` for the notification endpoint. An optional `payloadTemplate` can customize the data sent.

```JSON
[
  {
    "eventTypes": ["ACTOR.RUN.FAILED"],
    "requestUrl": "https://example.com/run-failed"
  },
  {
    "eventTypes": ["ACTOR.RUN.SUCCEEDED"],
    "requestUrl": "https://example.com/run-succeeded",
    "payloadTemplate": "{\"hello\": \"world\", \"resource\":{{resource}}}"
  }
]
```

- **eventTypes** (array of strings) - Required - The event types that will trigger the webhook (e.g., `ACTOR.RUN.FAILED`, `ACTOR.RUN.SUCCEEDED`).
- **requestUrl** (string) - Required - The URL to send the webhook request to.
- **payloadTemplate** (string) - Optional - A template for the request body payload. You can use Liquid syntax and variables like `{{resource}}`.
- **idempotencyKey** (string) - Optional - A unique key to prevent duplicate webhooks in case of Actor restarts. It's recommended to use the Actor run ID.
```

--------------------------------

### Ad-hoc Webhook Definition JSON

Source: https://docs.apify.com/platform/integrations/webhooks/ad-hoc-webhooks

Example of a JSON array defining ad-hoc webhooks. Each object specifies event types to listen for and the URL to send the notification to. Supports optional payload templates for custom data.

```json
[
    {
        "eventTypes": ["ACTOR.RUN.FAILED"],
        "requestUrl": "https://example.com/run-failed"
    },
    {
        "eventTypes": ["ACTOR.RUN.SUCCEEDED"],
        "requestUrl": "https://example.com/run-succeeded",
        "payloadTemplate": "{\"hello\": \"world\", \"resource\":{{resource}}}"
    }
]
```

--------------------------------

### JSON Structure for Ad-hoc Webhook Definitions

Source: https://docs.apify.com/platform/integrations/webhooks/ad-hoc-webhooks

This JSON structure defines the parameters for ad-hoc webhooks, including `eventTypes` to specify when the webhook should trigger and `requestUrl` for the notification endpoint. An optional `payloadTemplate` can customize the data sent.

```JSON
[
  {
    "eventTypes": ["ACTOR.RUN.FAILED"],
    "requestUrl": "https://example.com/run-failed"
  },
  {
    "eventTypes": ["ACTOR.RUN.SUCCEEDED"],
    "requestUrl": "https://example.com/run-succeeded",
    "payloadTemplate": "{\"hello\": \"world\", \"resource\":{{resource}}}"
  }
]
```

# Marketify — Ralph Agent Instructions

You are an autonomous coding agent building **Marketify**, a mobile marketplace where creators claim bounties from listers (Expo + React Native + Supabase). Each invocation is a fresh Claude Code instance with no prior memory. Every persistent decision lives in git history, `scripts/ralph/progress.txt`, and `scripts/ralph/prd.json`.

---

## Authoritative specs (read these for any ambiguity)

| File | What it defines |
|---|---|
| `docs/product-plan.md` | Canonical product, 12-step build sequence, scope cuts |
| `docs/design.md` | Visual language, component inventory, screen catalog, motion |
| `docs/tech-architecture.md` | DB schema (§4.7), RLS, triggers, Apify integration (§3), edge-function spec, eligibility engine |

If a story is ambiguous, the specs win. Do **not** invent product behavior the docs don't sanction. If something is genuinely missing, append a `Spec gap:` note to `progress.txt` and pick the simplest interpretation that aligns with the docs.

---

## The loop you run, every iteration

1. **Orient.** Read `scripts/ralph/prd.json` and `scripts/ralph/progress.txt`. Pay special attention to the `## Codebase Patterns` section at the top of `progress.txt` — it consolidates everything previous iterations learned.
2. **Branch.** Make sure you're on the branch named in `prd.json.branchName`. If it doesn't exist, create it from `main`.
3. **Pick one story.** Lowest `priority` number where `passes: false`. Work on **exactly one** story per iteration.
4. **Research before coding.** If understanding the story requires reading >3 files, **delegate to the Explore subagent** (`subagent_type: "Explore"`) so your own context stays clean for implementation. Pass it the story plus pointers into the docs.
5. **Implement.** Make the minimal change set that satisfies every acceptance criterion. Match existing conventions. Don't refactor adjacent code.
6. **Quality gates** (run in this order, stop on first failure and fix before continuing):
   - `npx tsc --noEmit` — must pass with zero errors
   - `npx expo lint` — must pass (warnings allowed only if pre-existing in main)
   - Migration sanity: any new SQL must apply cleanly via Supabase MCP without manual fix-up
7. **Code review (mandatory before commit).** Spawn the `feature-dev:code-reviewer` subagent with the diff scope, the story acceptance criteria, and the relevant doc sections. Address every issue rated medium or higher. Re-run quality gates after fixes.
8. **Mobile verification (mandatory for any UI story).** Use `mobile-mcp` to:
   - Launch the app on the iOS simulator (`mcp__mobile-mcp__mobile_launch_app`)
   - Navigate to the screen the story touches
   - Exercise the golden path
   - Take a screenshot (`mcp__mobile-mcp__mobile_take_screenshot`) and reference it in the progress note
   - If the simulator is unavailable, **do not silently skip** — set `passes: false`, write a `notes` entry on the story explaining the blocker, and move to the next story.
9. **Commit.** Stage everything you changed (no `git add -A` blanket — list paths). Commit message format:
   ```
   feat: [US-XXX] Story title
   ```
10. **Mark done.** In `prd.json`, set `passes: true` for the story you just finished. Append a dated entry to `progress.txt` (format below).
11. **Stop check.** If every story has `passes: true`, your final response must contain `<promise>COMPLETE</promise>` and nothing else useful. Otherwise end normally — the next iteration picks up.

---

## MCP servers — use these instead of CLI wherever possible

### Supabase (`mcp__plugin_supabase_supabase__*`) — required for ALL database work
- `apply_migration` — every schema change. Migrations are append-only and named after the story (e.g. `us_006_metric_snapshots_denorm_trigger`).
- `execute_sql` — read-only queries, RLS verification, seed data inspection.
- `list_tables`, `list_migrations`, `list_extensions` — orient before changing things.
- `generate_typescript_types` — regenerate `src/types/supabase.ts` after every migration. Commit the regenerated file with the migration.
- `deploy_edge_function`, `get_edge_function`, `list_edge_functions` — for `apify-core`, `auth-*`, `metrics-refresh`, the webhook receiver, etc.
- `get_advisors` — run with `type: "security"` after any schema or RLS change. Address every error-level advisor before committing.
- `get_logs` — when an edge function or query misbehaves.

Do **not** use the local `supabase` CLI for migrations or queries. The MCP is the source of truth so iterations stay consistent.

### Mobile (`mcp__mobile-mcp__*`) — required for any UI verification
- `mobile_list_available_devices` then `mobile_launch_app` to boot the app.
- `mobile_list_elements_on_screen` + `mobile_click_on_screen_at_coordinates` to drive flows.
- `mobile_take_screenshot` after each meaningful state — name screenshots `us-XXX-state.png` and store under `scripts/ralph/screenshots/`.
- Use `mobile_type_keys` rather than tapping virtual keys when filling text inputs.

### Context7 (`mcp__plugin_context7_context7__*`) — for any library docs
Use **before** writing non-trivial integration code with Expo, Reanimated, NativeWind, Supabase JS, React Navigation, etc. Your training data may be stale. `resolve-library-id` then `query-docs`.

### Apify
The API key is in `.env` as `APIFY_KEY`. Always read it at runtime via `process.env.APIFY_KEY` (or `Deno.env.get("APIFY_KEY")` inside edge functions). **Never** paste the key into source. Actor catalog and input schemas live in `docs/tech-architecture.md` §3.

---

## Subagent delegation — preserve your context

You will burn out your own context window if you try to do everything in-line. Delegate aggressively:

| When | Subagent | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Need to read >3 files to understand the codebase | `Explore` | Returns a focused summary instead of dumping raw file contents into your context |
| Before every commit | `feature-dev:code-reviewer` | Independent review against acceptance criteria + doc sections |
| Implementation will touch >5 files (e.g. wiring a new edge function end-to-end) | `feature-dev:code-architect` first, then implement yourself | Plan once, execute once |
| Need to research how something works before changing it | `feature-dev:code-explorer` | Maps the existing layers without polluting your context |
| Open-ended search across the repo | `general-purpose` | Iterates until it finds the answer |

When briefing a subagent, give it the story ID, acceptance criteria, and a pointer to the relevant `docs/*.md` section. Do **not** tell it "based on your findings, implement" — synthesize the result yourself.

---

## Project quality conventions (these become permanent — additions go in `progress.txt` Codebase Patterns)

- **TypeScript everywhere.** No `any` without a `// eslint-disable-next-line` and a comment explaining why. Generated Supabase types are imported from `src/types/supabase.ts`.
- **Migrations are append-only.** Never edit a previously applied migration. If you need to change something, write a new migration that alters the prior state.
- **Edge functions** live in `supabase/functions/<name>/index.ts`. Each one has a one-paragraph header comment naming the contract (request shape → response shape) and the auth requirement.
- **RLS is mandatory** on every table that holds user data. After applying any RLS migration, run `get_advisors({type: "security"})` and fix every finding before committing.
- **Component organization:** primitives in `src/components/primitives/`, screen-specific composites in `src/screens/<screen>/`, shared composites in `src/components/shared/`.
- **No emoji** anywhere in the product UI (per `docs/design.md` — Lucide icons only).
- **No payments code** in v1 — `price_cents` is display-only.
- **Dual-role accounts** are out of scope for v1. One role per account.

---

## Progress log format (`progress.txt`)

Append (never replace) using this format:

```
## YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM — US-XXX
- What was implemented (1–3 bullets, concrete)
- Files changed (bulleted list)
- Quality checks: tsc ✓ / lint ✓ / supabase advisors ✓ / mobile-mcp ✓
- Code-reviewer verdict: [approved | issues fixed in this commit]
- **Learnings for future iterations:**
  - Patterns discovered
  - Gotchas
  - Useful pointers
---
```

If a learning is **general and reusable across stories**, also promote it into the `## Codebase Patterns` section at the top of `progress.txt`. That section is what every future iteration leans on first — keep it tight, deduplicated, and signal-only.

---

## Stop condition

After updating `prd.json`, count remaining `passes: false` stories. If zero, your last line in this response must be exactly:

```
<promise>COMPLETE</promise>
```

Otherwise end normally and let the loop spawn the next iteration.

---

## Hard rules (do not violate)

- **One story per iteration.** Don't peek ahead.
- **Never commit broken code.** A failing typecheck means you don't commit, you don't mark `passes: true`, you write a `notes` entry, and the next iteration retries.
- **Never skip the code-reviewer subagent.** It is the second pair of eyes that keeps quality from drifting across iterations.
- **Never skip mobile verification on UI stories.** A screen "looking right in the JSX" is not the same as it actually rendering correctly.
- **Never read `.env` contents into the conversation.** Reference variable names only.
- **Never edit `docs/product-plan.md`, `docs/design.md`, or `docs/tech-architecture.md`** unless a story explicitly says to. They are specs, not work product.
- **Never invent stories** or add new ones to `prd.json` mid-loop. If you discover a missing piece, append it as a `Spec gap:` line in `progress.txt` so the human can decide.

## plugin:context7:context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

---
name: using-superpowers
description: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring Skill tool invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
---

<SUBAGENT-STOP>
If you were dispatched as a subagent to execute a specific task, skip this skill.
</SUBAGENT-STOP>

<EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>
If you think there is even a 1% chance a skill might apply to what you are doing, you ABSOLUTELY MUST invoke the skill.

IF A SKILL APPLIES TO YOUR TASK, YOU DO NOT HAVE A CHOICE. YOU MUST USE IT.

This is not negotiable. This is not optional. You cannot rationalize your way out of this.
</EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>

## Instruction Priority

Superpowers skills override default system prompt behavior, but **user instructions always take precedence**:

1. **User's explicit instructions** (CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, AGENTS.md, direct requests) — highest priority
2. **Superpowers skills** — override default system behavior where they conflict
3. **Default system prompt** — lowest priority

If CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, or AGENTS.md says "don't use TDD" and a skill says "always use TDD," follow the user's instructions. The user is in control.

## How to Access Skills

**In Claude Code:** Use the `Skill` tool. When you invoke a skill, its content is loaded and presented to you—follow it directly. Never use the Read tool on skill files.

**In Copilot CLI:** Use the `skill` tool. Skills are auto-discovered from installed plugins. The `skill` tool works the same as Claude Code's `Skill` tool.

**In Gemini CLI:** Skills activate via the `activate_skill` tool. Gemini loads skill metadata at session start and activates the full content on demand.

**In other environments:** Check your platform's documentation for how skills are loaded.

## Platform Adaptation

Skills use Claude Code tool names. Non-CC platforms: see `references/copilot-tools.md` (Copilot CLI), `references/codex-tools.md` (Codex) for tool equivalents. Gemini CLI users get the tool mapping loaded automatically via GEMINI.md.

# Using Skills

## The Rule

**Invoke relevant or requested skills BEFORE any response or action.** Even a 1% chance a skill might apply means that you should invoke the skill to check. If an invoked skill turns out to be wrong for the situation, you don't need to use it.

```dot
digraph skill_flow {
    "User message received" [shape=doublecircle];
    "About to EnterPlanMode?" [shape=doublecircle];
    "Already brainstormed?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke brainstorming skill" [shape=box];
    "Might any skill apply?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke Skill tool" [shape=box];
    "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'" [shape=box];
    "Has checklist?" [shape=diamond];
    "Create TodoWrite todo per item" [shape=box];
    "Follow skill exactly" [shape=box];
    "Respond (including clarifications)" [shape=doublecircle];

    "About to EnterPlanMode?" -> "Already brainstormed?";
    "Already brainstormed?" -> "Invoke brainstorming skill" [label="no"];
    "Already brainstormed?" -> "Might any skill apply?" [label="yes"];
    "Invoke brainstorming skill" -> "Might any skill apply?";

    "User message received" -> "Might any skill apply?";
    "Might any skill apply?" -> "Invoke Skill tool" [label="yes, even 1%"];
    "Might any skill apply?" -> "Respond (including clarifications)" [label="definitely not"];
    "Invoke Skill tool" -> "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'";
    "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'" -> "Has checklist?";
    "Has checklist?" -> "Create TodoWrite todo per item" [label="yes"];
    "Has checklist?" -> "Follow skill exactly" [label="no"];
    "Create TodoWrite todo per item" -> "Follow skill exactly";
}
```

## Red Flags

These thoughts mean STOP—you're rationalizing:

| Thought | Reality |
|---------|---------|
| "This is just a simple question" | Questions are tasks. Check for skills. |
| "I need more context first" | Skill check comes BEFORE clarifying questions. |
| "Let me explore the codebase first" | Skills tell you HOW to explore. Check first. |
| "I can check git/files quickly" | Files lack conversation context. Check for skills. |
| "Let me gather information first" | Skills tell you HOW to gather information. |
| "This doesn't need a formal skill" | If a skill exists, use it. |
| "I remember this skill" | Skills evolve. Read current version. |
| "This doesn't count as a task" | Action = task. Check for skills. |
| "The skill is overkill" | Simple things become complex. Use it. |
| "I'll just do this one thing first" | Check BEFORE doing anything. |
| "This feels productive" | Undisciplined action wastes time. Skills prevent this. |
| "I know what that means" | Knowing the concept ≠ using the skill. Invoke it. |

## Skill Priority

When multiple skills could apply, use this order:

1. **Process skills first** (brainstorming, debugging) - these determine HOW to approach the task
2. **Implementation skills second** (frontend-design, mcp-builder) - these guide execution

"Let's build X" → brainstorming first, then implementation skills.
"Fix this bug" → debugging first, then domain-specific skills.

## Skill Types

**Rigid** (TDD, debugging): Follow exactly. Don't adapt away discipline.

**Flexible** (patterns): Adapt principles to context.

The skill itself tells you which.

## User Instructions

Instructions say WHAT, not HOW. "Add X" or "Fix Y" doesn't mean skip workflows.


</EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>

lines: //   POST { username: string, tiktok_handle?: string, instagram_handle?: string }
+//         metrics_status: { tiktok?: Status, ig_details?: Status, ig_posts?: Status },
// Atomicity: the users + creator_profiles + social_links inserts run
// inside a single Postgres transaction via the public.auth_signup_creator
// RPC (us_019 migration). A failure on any insert rolls back the flow.
+// Apify dispatch happens AFTER the RPC commits — per spec §5.1, a
+// dispatch failure does NOT roll back the account (the user gets their
+// JWT, metrics_status carries the per-platform outcome so the client can
+// decide whether to retry later or surface a 'refreshing' placeholder).
// Handle normalization: leading '@' is stripped and whitespace trimmed
// before the RPC call. Handles are compared case-insensitively (citext),
-// Scope: this function only records the handles. Kicking the Apify
-// scrapes is deferred to US-020, which will wrap this handler.
+// Apify dispatch (US-020): once the RPC commits, we look up the
+// freshly-inserted social_links rows, then kick one or more Apify runs in
+// parallel via Promise.allSettled — tiktok_profile for TikTok handles;
+// ig_details and ig_posts for Instagram handles. Each run is registered
+// with a per-run webhook pointing back at /functions/v1/apify-webhook so
+// the receiver can persist the metric_snapshots row when Apify finishes.
+// The wait window is APIFY_WAIT_SECS (60s); runs that come back
+// SUCCEEDED within the window are reported as 'fresh' (the webhook will
+// also arrive and be deduped via the unique index on run_id), otherwise
+// 'refreshing' and the client polls. A thrown dispatch is 'failed'.
+// Pre-refreshing rows: the spec calls for pre-creating metric_snapshots
+// rows in status='refreshing' before the scrape so the client has
+// something to read. That's deferred — the existing US-016..US-018 RPCs
+// INSERT on webhook arrival via ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING, so the row
+// appears only once Apify finishes. Tracked as a spec gap in progress.txt.
// Rate limit: not implemented yet. Spec §5.1 requires per-IP throttling
// via a signup_attempts table (does not yet exist). Same status as the
// sibling auth-signup-lister — tracked in progress.txt Codebase Patterns.

content.content:
1	// Shared CORS headers for every Marketify edge function.
2	//
3	// Contract: exports `corsHeaders` (Record<string, string>) to splat into
4	//           every edge function's response and a pre-flight short-circuit.
5	// Auth:     n/a — the browser reads these before the request runs.
6	//
7	// `*` origin is safe here: edge functions authenticate via the
8	// Authorization: Bearer <marketify_jwt> header, not cookies, so there is no
9	// ambient-credential risk to guard against with a narrower origin allowlist.
10	
11	export const corsHeaders: Record<string, string> = {
12	  "Access-Control-Allow-Origin": "*",
13	  "Access-Control-Allow-Headers":
14	    "authorization, x-client-info, apikey, content-type",
15	  "Access-Control-Allow-Methods": "GET, POST, PATCH, DELETE, OPTIONS",
16	};
17

input.query:
select sl.platform, ms.scrape_mode, ms.status, ms.apify_run_id is not null as has_run_id
from social_links sl
left join metric_snapshots ms on ms.social_link_id = sl.id
where sl.user_id = '1342666e-babc-48a5-b742-921ecb50fa2e'
order by sl.platform, ms.scrape_mode;

content.content:
260	| `likesCount`, `commentsCount` | engagement | (stored) |
261	| `videoDuration` | seconds | (stored) |
262	| `caption`, `hashtags`, `mentions`, `timestamp`, `url`, `shortCode` | post meta | (stored) |
263	
264	**Metric derivation (Instagram):**
265	- `follower_count = details[0].followersCount`
266	- `media_count = details[0].postsCount`
267	- `avg_views_last_10 = mean(p.videoPlayCount ?? p.videoViewCount for p in posts where p.type === "Video")` — skip the profile entirely if there are fewer than 1 video posts (set `avg_views_last_10 = null`, surface in UI as "Not enough video posts to compute").
268	
269	**Photo-only creators:** if `posts` returns 0 `type === "Video"` entries across 10 results, we write `instagram_avg_views_last_10 = null` and surface it in the UI as "Not enough video posts to compute." **No fallback run to `resultsType: "reels"`** — the cost doubling (extra ~$0.007/refresh × every photo-only creator × daily cron) wasn't justified in back-of-envelope math vs. just telling the creator to post more reels. Listings that require `min_avg_views_last_n` on Instagram treat `null` as ineligible (fail-closed).
270	
271	**Two runs per IG refresh.** Budget: 1 run = TikTok refresh, 2 runs = IG refresh. No conditional third run.
272	
273	### 3d. Client Pattern
274	
275	```ts
276	import { ApifyClient } from 'apify-client';
277	const apify = new ApifyClient({ token: Deno.env.get('APIFY_TOKEN')! });
278	
279	// --- synchronous short-run (used on first signup — user is waiting) ---
280	const tiktok = await apify
281	  .actor('clockworks/tiktok-scraper')
282	  .call(
283	    {
284	      profiles: [handle],
285	      resultsPerPage: 10,
286	      profileScrapeSections: ['videos'],
287	      profileSorting: 'latest',
288	      excludePinnedPosts: true,
289	      shouldDownloadVideos: false,
290	      shouldDownloadCovers: false,
291	    },
292	    { waitSecs: 60 },
293	  );
294	const { items: tiktokItems } = await apify.dataset(tiktok.defaultDatasetId).listItems();
295	
296	// --- async run with webhook (used for cron refresh and initial IG scrapes) ---
297	const run = await apify
298	  .actor('apify/instagram-scraper')
299	  .start(
300	    {
301	      directUrls: [`https://www.instagram.com/${handle}/`],
302	      resultsType: 'details',
303	      resultsLimit: 1,
304	    },
305	    {
306	      webhooks: [{
307	        eventTypes: ['ACTOR.RUN.SUCCEEDED', 'ACTOR.RUN.FAILED', 'ACTOR.RUN.TIMED_OUT', 'ACTOR.RUN.ABORTED'],
308	        requestUrl: `${SUPABASE_URL}/functions/v1/webhooks-apify`,
309	        payloadTemplate: JSON.stringify({
310	          social_link_id: '<id>',
311	          scrape_mode: 'ig_details',       // or 'ig_posts' | 'tiktok_profile'
312	          run_id: '{{resource.id}}',
313	          status: '{{resource.status}}',
314	          dataset_id: '{{resource.defaultDatasetId}}',
315	        }),
316	        headersTemplate: JSON.stringify({
317	          'X-Apify-Webhook-Secret': Deno.env.get('APIFY_WEBHOOK_SECRET')!,
318	        }),
319	      }],
320	    },
321	  );
322	```
323	
324	**Signup flow (user waiting):** Use sync `.call(input, { waitSecs: 60 })` for the *first* scrape per handle. If the actor finishes inside 60s, populate the snapshot inline; if it doesn't, the `waitSecs` limit returns a `RUNNING` run and the webhook completes it later. UI shows "Fetching your metrics..." until either returns.
325	
326	**Cron refresh / manual refresh:** Always async (`.start()` with webhook). The refresh endpoint queues work and returns `202 Accepted` immediately.
327	
328	### 3e. Metric Derivation — Summary
329	
330	| Denormalized field on `creator_profiles` | TikTok source | Instagram source |
331	|---|---|---|
332	| `tiktok_follower_count` | `authorMeta.fans` | — |
333	| `tiktok_avg_views_last_10` | `mean(playCount[:10])` | — |
334	| `tiktok_video_count` | `authorMeta.video` | — |
335	| `tiktok_is_verified` | `authorMeta.verified` | — |
336	| `instagram_follower_count` | — | `details[0].followersCount` |
337	| `instagram_avg_views_last_10` | — | `mean(videoPlayCount ?? videoViewCount)` across `type === "Video"` posts |
338	| `instagram_media_count` | — | `details[0].postsCount` |
339	| `instagram_is_verified` | — | `details[0].verified` *(reserved; column add in v1.1)* |

content.text:
### String Interpolated Webhook Payload Template

Source: https://docs.apify.com/webhooks/actions

This payload template uses string interpolation to ensure the template itself is valid JSON while still allowing for dynamic data injection using the same variable syntax.

```json
{
  "userId": "{{userId}}",
  "createdAt": "{{createdAt}}",
  "eventType": "{{eventType}}",
  "eventData": "{{eventData}}",
  "resource": "{{resource}}"
}
```

--------------------------------

### Custom Payload Template for Apify Webhook

Source: https://docs.apify.com/platform/integrations/webhooks/actions

This example demonstrates how to use payload template variables to send a custom JSON object containing the status, ID, and a custom property of an Apify run. It utilizes resource variables like 'resource.id' and 'resource.status'.

```json
{
    "runId": {{resource.id}},
    "runStatus": {{resource.status}},
    "myProp": "hello world"
}
```

--------------------------------

### Payload Templating Example - Apify Webhooks

Source: https://docs.apify.com/webhooks/actions

This example demonstrates how to use payload template variables to send a custom JSON object containing the status and ID of an Apify run, along with a custom property. String interpolation is used to dynamically insert variable values.

```json
{
  "runId": {{resource.id}},
  "runStatus": {{resource.status}},
  "myProp": "hello world"
}
```

--------------------------------

### String Interpolation Example - Apify Webhooks

Source: https://docs.apify.com/webhooks/actions

Illustrates how string interpolation works in Apify webhooks. If a string field contains only a variable like `"{{eventData}}"`, the actual variable value is used. If the string contains more than just the variable, the string representation of the variable is embedded.

```json
{
  "text": "My user id is {{userId}}"
}
```

```json
{
  "text": "My user id is abf6vtB2nvQZ4nJzo"
}
```

Source: https://docs.apify.com/sdk/js/reference/interface/WebhookOptions

WebhookOptions<!-- --> > Properties<!-- -->[**](#Properties) > [**](#payloadTemplate)[**](https://github.com/apify/apify-sdk-js/blob/master/packages/apify/src/actor.ts#L1935)optionalpayloadTemplate: Payload template is a JSON-like string that describes the structure of the webhook POST request payload. It uses JSON syntax, extended with a double curly braces syntax for injecting variables `{{variable}}`. Those variables are resolved at the time of the webhook's dispatch, and a list of available variables with their descriptions is available in the [Apify webhook documentation](https://docs.apify.com/webhooks). If `payloadTemplate` is omitted, the default payload template is used ([view docs](https://docs.apify.com/webhooks/actions#payload-template)).

content.text: The docs say `"{{resource}}"` (placeholder-only string) substitutes as the raw object, not a quoted string. My nested field-by-field template didn't work — likely because `resource.actId` doesn't resolve via dot notation. Switching to the simpler `"{{resource}}"` pattern.

content.content: The file /Users/bulutyazgan/Desktop/marketify/supabase/functions/auth-signup-creator/index.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: -    // Every placeholder is quoted so the rendered output is valid JSON.
-    // plus our injected social_link_id / scrape_mode / run_id. See
-    // apify-webhook/index.ts validResource for the required shape.
+    // Apify's template substitution rule: when a JSON string value contains
+    // ONLY a single placeholder (e.g. "{{resource}}"), the placeholder is
+    // replaced with the raw JSON value — including objects. That lets us
+    // forward the full run resource in-place without enumerating every
+    // field. apify-webhook/index.ts validResource only needs id,
+    // defaultDatasetId, status, and actId; we pull them all from resource.

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   This is a continuation of the Ralph autonomous loop iteration for Marketify (mobile marketplace, Expo + React Native + Supabase). The single iteration task is **US-020 "Wire signup-creator to kick Apify scrapes (waitSecs=60)"**. Acceptance criteria:
   - Extend `supabase/functions/auth-signup-creator/index.ts` to call `runTikTokProfile` / `runInstagramDetails` / `runInstagramPosts` per handles supplied
   - Use `Promise.allSettled` — partial failure acceptable
   - Response includes `metrics_status: { tiktok, ig_details, ig_posts }` map
   - Test with real TikTok/IG handle and confirm `metric_snapshots` rows appear within 60s
   - Commit, update `prd.json`, append to `progress.txt`
   
   Per Ralph CLAUDE.md: spawn code-reviewer subagent before commit, use Supabase MCP for deploys, no new stories, never skip quality gates.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Supabase Edge Functions (Deno runtime)
   - Apify REST API per-run ad-hoc webhooks via `?webhooks=<base64-encoded-JSON-array>` query param
   - Apify payload template substitution semantics: `"{{placeholder}}"` with placeholder-only strings substitutes the **raw JSON value** (including objects); strings with extra text substitute as string representation
   - Apify actors: `clockworks~tiktok-scraper`, `apify~instagram-scraper`
   - `Promise.allSettled` for partial-failure-tolerant parallel dispatch
   - Supabase MCP: `apply_migration`, `deploy_edge_function`, `execute_sql`, `get_logs`, `list_projects`
   - PL/pgSQL RPCs with `unique_violation` → `constraint_name` error mapping
   - RLS with service-role client bypass
   - Dual-secret constant-time compare for webhook auth (`X-Apify-Webhook-Secret`)
   - Supabase project ID: `[REDACTED_SUPABASE_PROJECT_REF]`

3. Files and Code Sections:
   
   - **`supabase/functions/_shared/apify.ts`** (fully edited)
     - Added `ApifyWebhookSpec` interface: `{ eventTypes: string[], requestUrl: string, payloadTemplate: string, headersTemplate?: string }`
     - Extended `ApifyRunOptions` to include `webhooks?: ApifyWebhookSpec[]`
     - Modified `startActorRun` to accept webhooks param; serialize via `url.searchParams.set("webhooks", btoa(JSON.stringify(webhooks)))` when non-empty
     - Threaded `options.webhooks` through all 3 public wrappers
   
   - **`supabase/functions/_shared/apify.test.ts`** (added 3 tests)
     - "serializes webhooks option as base64 JSON in query param"
     - "omits webhooks query param when option is not provided"
     - "omits webhooks query param when option is empty array"
     - All 11 tests pass
   
   - **`supabase/functions/auth-signup-creator/index.ts`** (major extension)
     - Imports: `createClient, type SupabaseClient`, `type ApifyRunResult, type ApifyWebhookSpec, runInstagramDetails, runInstagramPosts, runTikTokProfile`
     - Constants: `APIFY_WAIT_SECS = 60`, `APIFY_TERMINAL_EVENTS`, `type MetricsKey = "tiktok" | "ig_details" | "ig_posts"`, `type MetricsStatus = "fresh" | "refreshing" | "failed"`
     - Added `dispatchApifyRuns` helper after successful JWT mint
     - Response shape now: `{ token, user_id, role: "creator", metrics_status }`
     - Final `buildWebhook` template (v3 deployed):
     ```ts
     payloadTemplate: JSON.stringify({
       eventType: "{{eventType}}",
       resource: "{{resource}}",
       social_link_id: linkId,
       scrape_mode: scrapeMode,
       run_id: "{{resource.id}}",
       dataset_id: "{{resource.defaultDatasetId}}",
     }),
     headersTemplate: JSON.stringify({
       "X-Apify-Webhook-Secret": webhookSecret,
     }),
     ```
     - Status mapping: `s === "SUCCEEDED" ? "fresh" : s === "RUNNING" || s === "READY" ? "refreshing" : "failed"`
     - Uses `SupabaseClient<any, any, any>` with `deno-lint-ignore no-explicit-any` for generic-widening mismatch
   
   - **`scripts/test/auth-signup-creator.ts`** (updated)
     - Added header note about Apify credit costs (~$0.08 per smoke run)
     - Added metrics_status assertion: accepts empty object OR object with valid `tiktok`/`ig_details`/`ig_posts` keys, values in `{"fresh","refreshing","failed"}`
   
   - **`scripts/test/auth-signup-creator-live.ts`** (newly created)
     - Uses real handles: `tiktok_handle: "tiktok"`, `instagram_handle: "nasa"`
     - Calls signup endpoint, asserts `metrics_status` has all 3 keys
     - Uses service role to verify metric_snapshots rows land, with retry up to 3 times x 5s
     - Cleans up test user via service-role DELETE

4. Errors and fixes:
   
   - **Deno type-check TS2322**: `ReturnType<typeof createClient>` didn't match inferred `SupabaseClient<any, "public", "public", any, any>`.
     - **Fix**: imported `type SupabaseClient` and typed as `SupabaseClient<any, any, any>` with `// deno-lint-ignore no-explicit-any` comment explaining generic-widening mismatch.
   
   - **Pre-existing `_shared/jwt.ts` TS2345** (SharedArrayBuffer type mismatch on `base64UrlDecode(sigB64)`):
     - Not introduced by this change; `npx tsc --noEmit` on RN side doesn't trip on this (only Deno check does). Left as-is.
   
   - **Webhook 400 failures after v2 deploy** (the major bug):
     - First smoke test showed all 3 platforms as `"fresh"` (Apify runs SUCCEEDED), but querying metric_snapshots returned 0 rows.
     - Edge function logs showed 3 webhook POSTs to `/functions/v1/apify-webhook` with 400 status.
     - Root cause: my initial template tried to build `resource` as a nested object field-by-field with `"{{resource.actId}}"` etc. The `actId` placeholder may not resolve via dot notation, OR nested object templating fails.
     - **Fix**: Per Apify docs from context7 query — when a JSON string value contains ONLY a single placeholder like `"{{resource}}"`, Apify substitutes the raw JSON value (including objects). Rewrote template to use `"resource": "{{resource}}"` for the whole object. Deployed as v3.
   
   - **`expo lint` exit-code confusion**: first runs produced no output (only env log). Confirmed `expo lint` only covers `/src /app /components` directories, not `/supabase` or `/scripts`. Explicitly ran `npx expo lint src app` and got exit 0.

5. Problem Solving:
   - Resolved Apify template substitution semantics via Context7 docs query; determined that `"{{resource}}"` pattern (placeholder-only string) substitutes raw JSON value including objects.
   - Designed metrics_status map to gracefully degrade when APIFY_KEY/APIFY_WEBHOOK_SECRET are absent (returns empty `{}` with a warn log).
   - Deferred pre-creating `refreshing` metric_snapshots rows (per spec §5.1) to future story — tracked as spec gap. Current webhook RPCs insert on arrival via ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING.
   - Dispatched webhooks synchronously after JWT mint; per §5.1 an Apify dispatch failure does NOT roll back the account.
   - **Still troubleshooting**: after v3 deploy, have not yet re-run the smoke test to verify webhooks now return 200 and metric_snapshots rows land.

6. All user messages:
   - No interactive user messages — this is an autonomous Ralph loop iteration.
   - A final system-generated message: "CRITICAL: Respond with TEXT ONLY. Do NOT call any tools... Your task is to create a detailed summary of the conversation so far..."
   - TodoWrite reminder system messages (multiple, telling me to update the todo list)

7. Pending Tasks:
   - Re-run smoke test against v3 deploy to verify webhooks now succeed
   - Verify metric_snapshots rows land for test user via `execute_sql`
   - Run live test `auth-signup-creator-live.ts` with real handles to confirm end-to-end DB writes
   - Spawn `feature-dev:code-reviewer` subagent on the diff (MANDATORY per Ralph CLAUDE.md)
   - Address any medium+ review issues, re-run quality gates
   - Commit (format `feat: [US-020] Wire signup-creator to kick Apify scrapes (waitSecs=60)`)
   - Update `scripts/ralph/prd.json` — set US-020 `passes: true`
   - Append dated entry to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt`, including any useful patterns for the Codebase Patterns section (especially the Apify `"{{resource}}"` template pattern learning — this is genuinely reusable across future Apify stories)

8. Current Work:
   Immediately before the summary request, I had just deployed **auth-signup-creator v3** with the corrected webhook template that uses `"resource": "{{resource}}"` for raw object substitution instead of the failing field-by-field nested-object construction. The deploy response confirmed version 3 is ACTIVE:
   ```
   {"id":"ec204431-3e05-4815-b4f4-1dd89c23cd8e","slug":"auth-signup-creator","version":3,"status":"ACTIVE",...}
   ```
   
   The diagnostic path was: v2 smoke test returned 200 with all `"fresh"` statuses, but logs showed 3 x 400 responses from apify-webhook immediately after. A context7 query to Apify docs revealed: `"If a string field contains only a variable like '{{eventData}}', the actual variable value is used"` — meaning `"{{resource}}"` substitutes the raw object. I rewrote buildWebhook accordingly and redeployed.

9. Optional Next Step:
   Re-run the smoke test to verify v3 fixes the 400-webhook issue, then query `metric_snapshots` for the test user to confirm rows land. From the last work before summary:
   
   > "Redeploying and re-running smoke test."
   > (deployed v3)
   
   Concretely: run `bun run scripts/test/auth-signup-creator.ts`, capture the new user_id, then via Supabase MCP `execute_sql`, query `select sl.platform, ms.scrape_mode, ms.status from social_links sl left join metric_snapshots ms on ms.social_link_id = sl.id where sl.user_id = '<new_user_id>'` to confirm 3 snapshot rows exist with status='fresh'. If that works, run `auth-signup-creator-live.ts` for real-handle verification, then proceed to the code-reviewer subagent and commit steps.

If you need specific details from before compaction (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /Users/bulutyazgan/.claude/projects/-Users-bulutyazgan-Desktop-marketify/7b7930aa-2676-46b4-8930-2ad02c7dd734.jsonl
Continue the conversation from where it left off without asking the user any further questions. Resume directly — do not acknowledge the summary, do not recap what was happening, do not preface with "I'll continue" or similar. Pick up the last task as if the break never happened.

## plugin:context7:context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

content:
<EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>
You have superpowers.

**Below is the full content of your 'superpowers:using-superpowers' skill - your introduction to using skills. For all other skills, use the 'Skill' tool:**

---
name: using-superpowers
description: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring Skill tool invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
---

<SUBAGENT-STOP>
If you were dispatched as a subagent to execute a specific task, skip this skill.
</SUBAGENT-STOP>

<EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>
If you think there is even a 1% chance a skill might apply to what you are doing, you ABSOLUTELY MUST invoke the skill.

IF A SKILL APPLIES TO YOUR TASK, YOU DO NOT HAVE A CHOICE. YOU MUST USE IT.

This is not negotiable. This is not optional. You cannot rationalize your way out of this.
</EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>

## Instruction Priority

Superpowers skills override default system prompt behavior, but **user instructions always take precedence**:

1. **User's explicit instructions** (CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, AGENTS.md, direct requests) — highest priority
2. **Superpowers skills** — override default system behavior where they conflict
3. **Default system prompt** — lowest priority

If CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, or AGENTS.md says "don't use TDD" and a skill says "always use TDD," follow the user's instructions. The user is in control.

## How to Access Skills

**In Claude Code:** Use the `Skill` tool. When you invoke a skill, its content is loaded and presented to you—follow it directly. Never use the Read tool on skill files.

**In Copilot CLI:** Use the `skill` tool. Skills are auto-discovered from installed plugins. The `skill` tool works the same as Claude Code's `Skill` tool.

**In Gemini CLI:** Skills activate via the `activate_skill` tool. Gemini loads skill metadata at session start and activates the full content on demand.

**In other environments:** Check your platform's documentation for how skills are loaded.

## Platform Adaptation

Skills use Claude Code tool names. Non-CC platforms: see `references/copilot-tools.md` (Copilot CLI), `references/codex-tools.md` (Codex) for tool equivalents. Gemini CLI users get the tool mapping loaded automatically via GEMINI.md.

# Using Skills

## The Rule

**Invoke relevant or requested skills BEFORE any response or action.** Even a 1% chance a skill might apply means that you should invoke the skill to check. If an invoked skill turns out to be wrong for the situation, you don't need to use it.

```dot
digraph skill_flow {
    "User message received" [shape=doublecircle];
    "About to EnterPlanMode?" [shape=doublecircle];
    "Already brainstormed?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke brainstorming skill" [shape=box];
    "Might any skill apply?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke Skill tool" [shape=box];
    "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'" [shape=box];
    "Has checklist?" [shape=diamond];
    "Create TodoWrite todo per item" [shape=box];
    "Follow skill exactly" [shape=box];
    "Respond (including clarifications)" [shape=doublecircle];

    "About to EnterPlanMode?" -> "Already brainstormed?";
    "Already brainstormed?" -> "Invoke brainstorming skill" [label="no"];
    "Already brainstormed?" -> "Might any skill apply?" [label="yes"];
    "Invoke brainstorming skill" -> "Might any skill apply?";

    "User message received" -> "Might any skill apply?";
    "Might any skill apply?" -> "Invoke Skill tool" [label="yes, even 1%"];
    "Might any skill apply?" -> "Respond (including clarifications)" [label="definitely not"];
    "Invoke Skill tool" -> "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'";
    "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'" -> "Has checklist?";
    "Has checklist?" -> "Create TodoWrite todo per item" [label="yes"];
    "Has checklist?" -> "Follow skill exactly" [label="no"];
    "Create TodoWrite todo per item" -> "Follow skill exactly";
}
```

## Red Flags

These thoughts mean STOP—you're rationalizing:

| Thought | Reality |
|---------|---------|
| "This is just a simple question" | Questions are tasks. Check for skills. |
| "I need more context first" | Skill check comes BEFORE clarifying questions. |
| "Let me explore the codebase first" | Skills tell you HOW to explore. Check first. |
| "I can check git/files quickly" | Files lack conversation context. Check for skills. |
| "Let me gather information first" | Skills tell you HOW to gather information. |
| "This doesn't need a formal skill" | If a skill exists, use it. |
| "I remember this skill" | Skills evolve. Read current version. |
| "This doesn't count as a task" | Action = task. Check for skills. |
| "The skill is overkill" | Simple things become complex. Use it. |
| "I'll just do this one thing first" | Check BEFORE doing anything. |
| "This feels productive" | Undisciplined action wastes time. Skills prevent this. |
| "I know what that means" | Knowing the concept ≠ using the skill. Invoke it. |

## Skill Priority

When multiple skills could apply, use this order:

1. **Process skills first** (brainstorming, debugging) - these determine HOW to approach the task
2. **Implementation skills second** (frontend-design, mcp-builder) - these guide execution

"Let's build X" → brainstorming first, then implementation skills.
"Fix this bug" → debugging first, then domain-specific skills.

## Skill Types

**Rigid** (TDD, debugging): Follow exactly. Don't adapt away discipline.

**Flexible** (patterns): Adapt principles to context.

The skill itself tells you which.

## User Instructions

Instructions say WHAT, not HOW. "Add X" or "Fix Y" doesn't mean skip workflows.


</EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>

content.content:
1	// Shared CORS headers for every Marketify edge function.
2	//
3	// Contract: exports `corsHeaders` (Record<string, string>) to splat into
4	//           every edge function's response and a pre-flight short-circuit.
5	// Auth:     n/a — the browser reads these before the request runs.
6	//
7	// `*` origin is safe here: edge functions authenticate via the
8	// Authorization: Bearer <marketify_jwt> header, not cookies, so there is no
9	// ambient-credential risk to guard against with a narrower origin allowlist.
10	
11	export const corsHeaders: Record<string, string> = {
12	  "Access-Control-Allow-Origin": "*",
13	  "Access-Control-Allow-Headers":
14	    "authorization, x-client-info, apikey, content-type",
15	  "Access-Control-Allow-Methods": "GET, POST, PATCH, DELETE, OPTIONS",
16	};
17

content.text: Let me directly test the webhook receiver with a manually-crafted payload (simulating what a proper Apify webhook would look like).

input.command:
source .env && curl -s -X POST "${SUPABASE_URL}/functions/v1/apify-webhook" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "X-Apify-Webhook-Secret: ${APIFY_WEBHOOK_SECRET}" \
  -d '{
    "eventType": "ACTOR.RUN.SUCCEEDED",
    "resource": {
      "id": "fake_run_1234",

content.text:
### Define Custom Webhook Payload with Resource Variables

Source: https://github.com/apify/apify-docs/blob/master/sources/platform/integrations/programming/webhooks/actions.md

An example of a custom payload template that extracts specific properties from the resource object, such as run ID and status.

```json5
{
    "runId": {{resource.id}},
    "runStatus": {{resource.status}},
    "myProp": "hello world"
}
```

--------------------------------

### Webhook Configuration Templating

Source: https://github.com/apify/apify-docs/blob/master/sources/platform/integrations/programming/webhooks/actions.md

Details on how to structure payload and header templates using Apify's variable interpolation system.

```APIDOC
## Webhook Payload and Header Templating

### Description
Configure dynamic webhook payloads and headers using string interpolation. This allows you to inject event-specific data directly into your JSON structures.

### Configuration
- **shouldInterpolateStrings** (boolean) - Enable to allow variable substitution within string fields.

### Available Variables
- **userId** (string) - ID of the Apify user.
- **createdAt** (string) - ISO timestamp of the event.
- **eventType** (string) - The type of trigger event.
- **eventData** (Object) - Event-specific data payload.
- **resource** (Object) - The resource triggering the event (e.g., Actor run or build).
- **globals** (Object) - Global context including dateISO and dateUnix.

### Payload Template Example
```json
{
    "runId": {{resource.id}},
    "runStatus": "{{resource.status}}",
    "myProp": "hello world"
}
```

### Headers Template
- The headers template must result in a valid JSON object where all values are strings.
- System-reserved headers (Host, Content-Type, X-Apify-*) cannot be overridden.

### Response
#### Success Response (200)
- **status** (string) - Webhook configuration successfully updated.
```

--------------------------------

### Create and Configure Webhooks

Source: https://context7.com/apify/apify-docs/llms.txt

Explains how to set up webhooks to receive HTTP POST notifications for actor events like success or failure. Supports custom payload templates for dynamic data injection.

```bash
curl -X POST "https://api.apify.com/v2/webhooks" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "requestUrl": "https://your-server.com/webhook",
    "eventTypes": ["ACTOR.RUN.SUCCEEDED", "ACTOR.RUN.FAILED"],
    "condition": {
      "actorId": "moJRLRc85AitArpNN"
    },
    "payloadTemplate": "{\"runId\": {{resource.id}}, \"status\": \"{{resource.status}}\", \"datasetId\": \"{{resource.defaultDatasetId}}\"}",
    "description": "Notify on scraper completion"
  }'
```

```javascript
import { ApifyClient } from 'apify-client';

const client = new ApifyClient({ token: 'YOUR_API_TOKEN' });

const webhook = await client.webhooks().create({
    requestUrl: 'https://your-api.com/apify-webhook',
    eventTypes: [
        'ACTOR.RUN.SUCCEEDED',
        'ACTOR.RUN.FAILED',
        'ACTOR.RUN.ABORTED'
    ],
    condition: {
        actorId: 'apify/web-scraper'
    },
    payloadTemplate: JSON.stringify({
        runId: '{{resource.id}}',
        status: '{{resource.status}}',
        datasetId: '{{resource.defaultDatasetId}}',
        stats: '{{resource.stats}}'
    }),
    shouldInterpolateStrings: true,
    description: 'Slack notification for scraper runs'
});

console.log(`Webhook created: ${webhook.id}`);
```

### Integrations > Webhooks > Actions > Payload template

Source: https://github.com/apify/apify-docs/blob/master/sources/platform/integrations/programming/webhooks/actions.md

The payload template is a JSON-like string that allows you to define a custom payload structure and inject dynamic data known only at the time of the webhook's invocation. Apart from the variables, the string must be a valid JSON. Variables must be enclosed in double curly braces and can only use the pre-defined variables listed in the [Available variables](#available-variables) section. Using any other variable will result in a validation error. The syntax of a variable is: `{{oneOfAvailableVariables}}`. Variables support accessing nested properties using dot notation: `{{variable.property}}`.

--------------------------------

### How templates work > Template syntax

Source: https://github.com/apify/apify-docs/blob/master/sources/platform/actors/development/actor_definition/output_schema/index.md

Templates use double curly braces `{{variable}}` for variable interpolation. You can access nested properties using dot notation (e.g., `{{run.defaultDatasetId}}`, `{{links.publicRunUrl}}`). For example, `{{links.apiDefaultDatasetUrl}}/items` becomes `https://api.apify.com/v2/datasets/<dataset-id>/items`, and `{{run.containerUrl}}` becomes `https://<container-id>.runs.apify.net/`.

content.text: Found it. The docs confirm dot notation is supported but **only with `shouldInterpolateStrings: true`** for strings containing placeholders. Without it, only bare unquoted `{{resource}}` substitutes. Let me verify this applies to ad-hoc webhooks too.

input.query: ad-hoc webhook actor run endpoint webhooks query parameter base64 spec fields shouldInterpolateStrings

content.text:
### POST /v2/acts/{actorId}/runs Source: https://github.com/apify/apify-docs/blob/master/sources/platform/integrations/programming/webhooks/ad_hoc_webhooks.md Starts an Actor run with ad-hoc webhooks defined via query parameters. ```APIDOC ## POST /v2/acts/{actorId}/runs ### Description Starts a new Actor run and attaches one-time webhooks that trigger on specific state transitions. ### Method POST ### Endpoint https://api.apify.com/v2/acts/{actorId}/runs?token={token}&webhooks={encoded_json} ### Parameters #### Path Parameters - **actorId** (string) - Required - The ID of the Actor to run. #### Query Parameters - **token** (string) - Required - Your Apify API token. - **webhooks** (string) - Required - A base64 encoded stringified JSON array of webhook objects. ### Request Example // webhooks parameter value (before base64 encoding): [ { "eventTypes": ["ACTOR.RUN.SUCCEEDED"], "requestUrl": "https://example.com/webhook" } ] ### Response #### Success Response (201) - **data** (object) - The details of the created Actor run. ``` -------------------------------- ### POST /acts/{actorId}/runs Source: https://github.com/apify/apify-docs/blob/master/sources/academy/tutorials/api/using_apify_from_php.md Triggers an Actor run. Supports synchronous waiting via query parameters or asynchronous notification via webhooks. ```APIDOC ## POST /acts/{actorId}/runs ### Description Starts a new run of the specified Actor. You can configure the request to wait for completion or trigger a webhook upon success. ### Method POST ### Endpoint /acts/{actorId}/runs ### Parameters #### Path Parameters - **actorId** (string) - Required - The ID or name of the Actor to run. #### Query Parameters - **waitForFinish** (integer) - Optional - Number of seconds to wait for the run to finish before responding. - **webhooks** (string) - Optional - Base64-encoded JSON string containing webhook event configurations. #### Request Body - **input** (object) - Optional - The input data for the Actor run. ### Request Example { "htmlString": "

Hi World

" } ### Response #### Success Response (200) - **data** (object) - Contains the run details including the run ID. #### Response Example { "data": { "id": "run-id-123", "status": "SUCCEEDED" } } ``` -------------------------------- ### POST /v2/webhooks Source: https://github.com/apify/apify-docs/blob/master/sources/platform/integrations/actors/integrating_actors_via_api.md This endpoint allows you to create a webhook. For Actor integration, specific fields like `requestUrl`, `payloadTemplate`, `shouldInterpolateStrings`, and `isApifyIntegration` must be configured correctly. ```APIDOC ## POST /v2/webhooks ### Description Creates a webhook for integrating Actors and tasks. Special configuration is required for Apify Console integration. ### Method POST ### Endpoint /v2/webhooks ### Parameters #### Request Body - **requestUrl** (string) - Required - The URL to which the webhook payload will be sent. For Actor integration, this should point to the 'Run Actor' or 'Run task' endpoints using their IDs. - **eventTypes** (array of strings) - Required - An array of event types that trigger the webhook (e.g., ["ACTOR.RUN.SUCCEEDED"]). - **condition** (object) - Optional - Conditions that must be met for the webhook to trigger. - **actorId** (string) - Required within condition - The ID of the Actor to monitor. - **shouldInterpolateStrings** (boolean) - Required - Set to `true` to enable variable interpolation in `payloadTemplate`. - **isApifyIntegration** (boolean) - Required - Set to `true` to enable the Actor integration UI in Apify Console. - **payloadTemplate** (string) - Required - A JSON string template for the webhook payload. Must contain a `payload` field and use variables enclosed in strings (e.g., `"{{variable}}"`). ### Request Example ```json { "requestUrl": "https://api.apify.com/v2/acts//runs", "eventTypes": ["ACTOR.RUN.SUCCEEDED"], "condition": { "actorId": "" }, "shouldInterpolateStrings": true, "isApifyIntegration": true, "payloadTemplate": "{\"field\":\"value\",\"payload\":{\"resource\":\"{{resource}}\"}}" } ``` ### Response #### Success Response (200) - **id** (string) - The unique identifier of the created webhook. - **requestUrl** (string) - The configured request URL. - **eventTypes** (array of strings) - The configured event types. - **condition** (object) - The configured condition. - **shouldInterpolateStrings** (boolean) - The configured interpolation setting. - **isApifyIntegration** (boolean) - The configured integration setting. - **payloadTemplate** (string) - The configured payload template. #### Response Example ```json { "id": "webhook-id-123", "requestUrl": "https://api.apify.com/v2/acts//runs", "eventTypes": ["ACTOR.RUN.SUCCEEDED"], "condition": { "actorId": "" }, "shouldInterpolateStrings": true, "isApifyIntegration": true, "payloadTemplate": "{\"field\":\"value\",\"payload\":{\"resource\":\"{{resource}}\"}}" } ``` ``` ### Integrations > Webhooks > Ad-hoc webhooks Source: https://github.com/apify/apify-docs/blob/master/sources/platform/integrations/programming/webhooks/ad_hoc_webhooks.md An ad-hoc webhook is a single-use webhook created for a specific Actor run when starting the run using the [Apify API](/api/v2). The webhook triggers once when the run transitions to the specified state. Define ad-hoc webhooks using the `webhooks` URL parameter added to the API endpoint that starts an Actor or Actor task. Replace `AD_HOC_WEBHOOKS` with a base64 encoded stringified JSON array of webhook definitions. -------------------------------- ### Integrating Actors via API Source: https://github.com/apify/apify-docs/blob/master/sources/platform/integrations/actors/integrating_actors_via_api.md You can integrate Actors via API using the Create webhook endpoint. It's the same as any other webhook, but to make sure you see it in Apify Console, you need to make sure of a few things. * The `requestUrl` field needs to point to the **Run Actor** or **Run task** endpoints and needs to use their IDs as identifiers (i.e. not their technical names). * The `payloadTemplate` field should be valid JSON - i.e. it should only use variables enclosed in strings. You will also need to make sure that it contains a `payload` field. * The `shouldInterpolateStrings` field needs to be set to `true`, otherwise the variables won't work. * Add `isApifyIntegration` field with the value `true`. This is a helper that turns on the Actor integration UI, if the above conditions are met. Not meeting the conditions does not mean that the webhook won't work; it will just be displayed as a regular HTTP webhook in Apify Console.

content.text: Root cause found: missing `shouldInterpolateStrings: true`. Without it, Apify treats `"{{resource}}"` as literal text and the receiver fails `validResource` because `body.resource` is a string not an object. Let me fix.

content.content: The file /Users/bulutyazgan/Desktop/marketify/supabase/functions/auth-signup-creator/index.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: -    // Apify's template substitution rule: when a JSON string value contains
-    // ONLY a single placeholder (e.g. "{{resource}}"), the placeholder is
-    // replaced with the raw JSON value — including objects. That lets us
-    // forward the full run resource in-place without enumerating every
-    // field. apify-webhook/index.ts validResource only needs id,
-    // defaultDatasetId, status, and actId; we pull them all from resource.
+    // Build a `resource` object field-by-field using dot-notation
+    // placeholders. Apify only substitutes placeholders that appear inside
+    // quoted JSON string values when `shouldInterpolateStrings: true`; bare
+    // unquoted `{{var}}` substitutes the raw JSON value but can't round-trip
+    // through JSON.stringify. Sticking to the quoted form keeps the template
+    // valid JSON before substitution and readable by any linter.
+    // status, and actId on the resource object.

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   This is a continuation of an autonomous Ralph loop iteration for **US-020 "Wire signup-creator to kick Apify scrapes (waitSecs=60)"** for Marketify (mobile marketplace MVP, Expo + React Native + Supabase). Acceptance criteria:
   - Extend `supabase/functions/auth-signup-creator/index.ts` to call `runTikTokProfile` / `runInstagramDetails` / `runInstagramPosts` per handles supplied
   - Use `Promise.allSettled` — partial failure acceptable
   - Response includes `metrics_status: { tiktok, ig_details, ig_posts }` map
   - Verify `metric_snapshots` rows appear within 60s via real handle test
   - Run code-reviewer subagent, commit, update `prd.json`, append to `progress.txt`
   
   Per Ralph CLAUDE.md: MANDATORY to spawn code-reviewer subagent before commit, use Supabase MCP for deploys, no new stories, never skip quality gates.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Supabase Edge Functions (Deno runtime)
   - Apify REST API ad-hoc webhooks via `?webhooks=<base64-encoded-JSON-array>`
   - **Apify payloadTemplate substitution rules**:
     - Bare unquoted `{{var}}` substitutes raw JSON value (object/array/etc) — but can't round-trip through `JSON.stringify`
     - Quoted `"{{var}}"` requires `shouldInterpolateStrings: true` to be substituted
     - Dot notation `{{resource.id}}` supported for nested field access
   - Apify actors: `clockworks~tiktok-scraper`, `apify~instagram-scraper`
   - `Promise.allSettled` for partial-failure-tolerant parallel dispatch
   - Supabase MCP tools: `deploy_edge_function`, `execute_sql`, `get_logs` (HTTP events only, not application console.log)
   - Constant-time dual-secret webhook auth
   - Context7 MCP for authoritative Apify docs

3. Files and Code Sections:
   
   - **`supabase/functions/_shared/apify.ts`** — Added `shouldInterpolateStrings?: boolean` to `ApifyWebhookSpec`:
     ```ts
     export interface ApifyWebhookSpec {
       eventTypes: string[];
       requestUrl: string;
       payloadTemplate: string;
       headersTemplate?: string;
       shouldInterpolateStrings?: boolean;
     }
     ```
     Also extended docstring to explain Apify's quoted-vs-bare placeholder rule.
   
   - **`supabase/functions/_shared/apify.test.ts`** — Added `shouldInterpolateStrings: true` to the webhook round-trip test. All 11 tests pass.
   
   - **`supabase/functions/auth-signup-creator/index.ts`** — Rewrote `buildWebhook` template to:
     ```ts
     payloadTemplate: JSON.stringify({
       eventType: "{{eventType}}",
       resource: {
         id: "{{resource.id}}",
         defaultDatasetId: "{{resource.defaultDatasetId}}",
         status: "{{resource.status}}",
         actId: "{{resource.actId}}",
       },
       social_link_id: linkId,
       scrape_mode: scrapeMode,
       run_id: "{{resource.id}}",
       dataset_id: "{{resource.defaultDatasetId}}",
     }),
     headersTemplate: JSON.stringify({
       "X-Apify-Webhook-Secret": webhookSecret,
     }),
     shouldInterpolateStrings: true,
     ```
     Resource object built field-by-field because JSON.stringify forces quotes. The `shouldInterpolateStrings: true` flag enables substitution inside quoted strings.
   
   - **`supabase/functions/apify-webhook/index.ts`** — Temporarily added debug logging (console.error + `_debug_body` in response); then reverted to clean `return jsonResponse(400, { error: "INVALID_REQUEST" });` after root cause was found via docs query. The receiver's `validResource` requires object with id/defaultDatasetId/status/actId as non-empty strings.
   
   - **`/tmp/deploy_creator.json`** — Built deploy manifest (23,804 bytes) containing index.ts, ../_shared/apify.ts, ../_shared/cors.ts, ../_shared/jwt.ts for the upcoming auth-signup-creator deploy.

4. Errors and fixes:
   
   - **Webhook 400 INVALID_REQUEST (the core bug)**: After v3 of auth-signup-creator deployed, smoke test returned 200 with all `"fresh"` but `metric_snapshots` stayed empty. Logs showed 3×400 from apify-webhook. 
     - **Diagnosis path**: (1) Manual curl with well-formed body → receiver returned 500 APIFY_FETCH_ERROR (proving receiver OK). (2) Added debug logging + redeployed → get_logs only returns HTTP events, not console.error. (3) Queried context7 Apify docs → found `shouldInterpolateStrings` is required for quoted-string placeholder substitution.
     - **Root cause**: My payloadTemplate had `"{{resource}}"` as a quoted string. Without `shouldInterpolateStrings: true`, Apify left it as literal text `"{{resource}}"` (a string, not an object), so the receiver's `validResource` check failed and returned 400.
     - **Fix**: Added `shouldInterpolateStrings: true` to spec, rebuilt resource as field-by-field object with dot-notation placeholders.
   
   - **metric_snapshots column mismatch**: First SQL query used `ms.created_at` and `ms.scraped_at` — both wrong. Correct column is `ms.fetched_at`. Resolved by introspecting `information_schema.columns`.

5. Problem Solving:
   Solved the Apify webhook 400 failure by correctly identifying that Apify's default payloadTemplate behavior does NOT substitute quoted `"{{var}}"` placeholders. The authoritative docs confirmed `shouldInterpolateStrings: true` is required. This is now a reusable learning for any future Apify webhook integration in the project.

6. All user messages:
   - No interactive user messages — autonomous Ralph loop.
   - Multiple `<system-reminder>` messages about TodoWrite state
   - "Tool loaded." confirmations after ToolSearch calls
   - Final: "CRITICAL: Respond with TEXT ONLY. Do NOT call any tools..." — the summary request.

7. Pending Tasks:
   - **Deploy apify-webhook v10** (without debug logging) and **auth-signup-creator v4** (with shouldInterpolateStrings fix) via Supabase MCP
   - Re-run `bun run scripts/test/auth-signup-creator.ts` to verify webhooks now return 200
   - Query `metric_snapshots` via `execute_sql` to confirm 3 rows per test user
   - Run `bun run scripts/test/auth-signup-creator-live.ts` with real TikTok/IG handles for full e2e verification
   - Spawn `feature-dev:code-reviewer` subagent on the diff (MANDATORY per Ralph CLAUDE.md)
   - Address any medium+ review issues, re-run quality gates (deno test, expo lint, typecheck)
   - Commit with message `feat: [US-020] Wire signup-creator to kick Apify scrapes (waitSecs=60)`
   - Update `scripts/ralph/prd.json` — set US-020 `passes: true`
   - Append dated entry to `scripts/ralph/progress.txt` including Apify template-substitution learning (`shouldInterpolateStrings: true` is required for quoted placeholders — reusable across future Apify stories)

8. Current Work:
   Immediately before the summary request, I had just:
   - Confirmed all 11 deno tests pass (with the updated webhook spec test)
   - Built the compact deploy manifest JSON at `/tmp/deploy_creator_compact.json` (23,804 bytes) containing `index.ts`, `../_shared/apify.ts`, `../_shared/cors.ts`, `../_shared/jwt.ts`
   - Read the manifest content to prepare the next `mcp__plugin_supabase_supabase__deploy_edge_function` call for auth-signup-creator
   
   The apify-webhook also needs redeployment (to remove the temporary debug echo of the body in the 400 response). The webhook source at `supabase/functions/apify-webhook/index.ts` is already reverted in the filesystem but deployed version 9 still has the debug `_debug_body` field.

9. Optional Next Step:
   Deploy both edge functions via Supabase MCP, then re-run smoke test. Per the todo list: "Redeploy both functions and re-run smoke test" (in_progress).
   
   Direct quote from the most recent work context:
   > "Now deploy both functions. I'll read the current auth-signup-creator entire file and also the jwt.ts shared file to build the deploy manifest."
   
   And:
   > "Root cause found: missing `shouldInterpolateStrings: true`. Without it, Apify treats `"{{resource}}"` as literal text and the receiver fails `validResource` because `body.resource` is a string not an object. Let me fix."
   
   Concretely the next action is: call `mcp__plugin_supabase_supabase__deploy_edge_function` for `auth-signup-creator` with the 4-file manifest (reading from `/tmp/deploy_creator_compact.json`), then deploy `apify-webhook` (single file, with the debug echo removed), then `bun run scripts/test/auth-signup-creator.ts`, then via `execute_sql` query `select sl.platform, ms.scrape_mode, ms.status from social_links sl left join metric_snapshots ms on ms.social_link_id = sl.id where sl.user_id = '<new_user_id>'` to confirm 3 snapshot rows land.

If you need specific details from before compaction (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /Users/bulutyazgan/.claude/projects/-Users-bulutyazgan-Desktop-marketify/7b7930aa-2676-46b4-8930-2ad02c7dd734.jsonl
Continue the conversation from where it left off without asking the user any further questions. Resume directly — do not acknowledge the summary, do not recap what was happening, do not preface with "I'll continue" or similar. Pick up the last task as if the break never happened.
